1: Welcome to introducing solids. Here we calmly go through how you let your baby discover food sometime between four and six months. Everything happens at your own pace, and Marie is with you the whole way.
2: We start with how you see that your baby is ready, and then move on to the first tastes. We talk about allergens, iron, and what you avoid, about the spoon or self-feeding, and about how you keep meals fun. Stop and ask whenever you like.
3: It is not the age on the calendar that decides, but that the body is ready. Look for several signs at once: that your baby holds the head steady, can sit with support, becomes curious about your food, and brings things to the mouth on their own. Marie: don't start before four months, and rather wait an extra week than the other way around.
4: In the beginning it is about taste and getting used to it, not about amount. A teaspoon or two is enough, if your baby only tastes a little the meal is a success anyway. The milk, that is breastfeeding or formula, is still the real food and carries on just as usual.
5: It is good to start with soft, mild flavours, mashed vegetables and root vegetables, soft fruit, and a smooth, iron-fortified porridge. Introduce one new thing at a time with a few days in between, so you easily see what suits. Season mildly and without salt; your baby is welcome to taste what you eat yourselves.
6: Don't wait with the common allergens. Introducing well-cooked egg and peanut, for example, as a smooth paste, never a whole nut, early and then regularly can actually reduce the risk of allergy. Marie: with known allergy in the family or troublesome eczema, check with the child health centre (BVC) or a doctor first.
7: Some things you save for later. No honey at all before one year of age, and go easy on salt and sugar. Whole nuts and hard pieces you grind instead, since they are choking risks, and unpasteurised things like raw milk and certain mould cheeses you wait with. Marie adjusts the list against your most recent advice.
8: Around six months your baby's own iron stores begin to run low, so the food then becomes an important source of iron. Offer iron-rich food such as meat, lentils, and fortified porridge, ideally together with fruit and vegetables that help the uptake. Marie verifies the iron and vitamin D advice against your routines.
9: There are two common paths. With a spoon and purée you feed your baby and find it easy to see the amount, and with self-feeding, baby-led weaning, your baby holds soft pieces and practises grip and pace. It works perfectly well to mix the two, choose what feels calm for you, but always make sure the food is soft and that your baby is sitting upright.
10: It is easy to get scared in the beginning, but gagging and choking are not the same thing. A baby coughing and gagging is a protective reflex, stay calm and let your baby work it through. Serve soft food in suitable sizes, sit together, and never leave your baby alone; for real choking you act according to infant resuscitation and call 112.
11: Food should be something fun, not a struggle. Making a mess and exploring is part of the learning, and one day your baby eats everything while the next day is leaner, both are normal. Nagging and forcing don't work; be a role model, eat yourselves, and let your baby set the pace.
12: Taste by taste you find your way together. Trust your baby, start gently, and be patient with the mess. What questions do you have?
